title: Tiny Teeth Tell the Story of Our First Primate Family
summary: Scientists found tiny teeth fossils in Colorado that belong to Purgatorius, the earliest known relative of all primates, including humans. These teeth are smaller than a fingertip and were discovered in a new southern location, helping fill a gap in the fossil record. Purgatorius lived about 65.9 million years ago, right after the dinosaurs went extinct, and was a small mammal like a shrew that likely lived in trees. Before this, fossils were only found in Montana and Canada, so this discovery shows that primates might have spread southward from the north. The research team used a special washing process to find these small fossils, with help from students and volunteers. This finding helps us understand how our primate ancestors evolved and recovered after a big asteroid impact.

How old are the Purgatorius fossils?
Where were Purgatorius fossils found before this discovery?
How did scientists find the tiny fossils?

background_read: Long ago, after dinosaurs disappeared, small mammals like Purgatorius started to evolve. Scientists study fossils to learn about these ancient animals and how they lived. Fossils are like clues from the past, found in rocks. This discovery helps us understand the early days of primates, which are animals like monkeys and humans. By finding tiny teeth in Colorado, researchers can piece together how these creatures spread across North America.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists led by Dr. Stephen Chester and a team from museums and colleges
WHAT: Discovered tiny teeth fossils of Purgatorius, the earliest primate relative
WHERE: In Colorado's Denver Basin at Corral Bluffs
WHY: To fill a gap in the fossil record and understand how primates evolved and spread

perspectives.description: Finding new fossils helps scientists learn more about ancient life and evolution
